data.title: US-Iran negotiations fall through; Bitcoin battles to defend the $70,000 mark.
data.description:
Author: Ma He, Foresight News

A major rebound in the crypto market remains a long way off. On April 13, the price of BTC hovered around $71,000, down about 2.6% in 24 hours. ETH performed even worse, falling 3.63% to about $2,200. Bitcoin's market capitalization still accounts for 58.8%, and the overall trading volume in the crypto sector has not increased significantly.

Coinglass data shows that in the past 24 hours, the total liquidation amount across the network was $284 million, with long positions liquidated at $203 million. Meanwhile, the CoinMarketCap fear index is currently at 43, indicating a neutral market sentiment.

Traditional financial markets also experienced significant volatility. Affected by the situation in the Middle East, international oil prices remained high, with Brent crude oil once approaching $107 per barrel, a sharp increase from pre-conflict levels. In the US stock market, the S&P 500 fell by approximately 5% in March, one of its worst monthly performances in recent years; international stock markets, dragged down by energy import pressures and a stronger dollar, saw even larger declines. Rising bond yields and renewed inflation expectations led investors to rapidly switch between risky assets, pushing up demand for traditional safe-haven assets such as gold. This cross-market correlation further confirms that cryptocurrencies, as high-beta risk assets, are simultaneously experiencing both macroeconomic and geopolitical shocks.

US-Iran Talks Break Down

One ​​of the key drivers of this week's sluggish cryptocurrency market was the sharp escalation of tensions between the US and Iran. The face-to-face peace talks between the US and Iran, held in Islamabad, Pakistan on April 11-12, lasted over 20 hours but ultimately ended in failure.

US Vice President Vance's delegation and the Iranian delegation failed to reach an agreement on core differences, including Iran's "red line" of abandoning its nuclear weapons program and halting uranium enrichment. Iran, in turn, accused the US of "maximumism" and "constantly changing its objectives."

Following the breakdown of negotiations, US President Trump announced on social media on Sunday that the US military would immediately begin a blockade of the Strait of Hormuz. US Central Command (CENTCOM) subsequently confirmed that at 10 PM Beijing time on April 13, the blockade would target all ships entering and leaving Iranian ports, but would not obstruct normal passage through non-Iranian ports. Iran's foreign minister and military quickly responded, warning that any military vessels approaching the strait would be considered a violation of the ceasefire and reserving the right to retaliate. The Strait of Hormuz is a vital chokepoint for global oil transportation, handling approximately 20% of the world's crude oil shipments. If the blockade continues, the risk of supply chain disruptions will directly push up oil prices, further triggering global inflation concerns and expectations of slower economic growth. Such geopolitical black swan events have the most direct impact on risk assets: investors are withdrawing from high-beta assets and turning to cash or gold. BTC's safe-haven properties as "digital gold" are suppressed by risk aversion in the short term, failing to play a hedging role. In past Middle East crises, the crypto market often reacted earlier than traditional markets, experiencing sharp corrections, which is a direct reflection of the current downturn. According to the latest data from Polymarket, the probability of the US and Iran reaching a permanent peace agreement before May 31st this year is 27%, while the probability of reaching an agreement before the 30th of this month has dropped to 14%. However, the future of US-Iran negotiations remains uncertain. According to the Wall Street Journal, informed officials revealed that Middle Eastern countries are vying to bring the United States and Iran back to the negotiating table after marathon peace talks in Islamabad failed to reach an agreement. Despite strong statements from both sides, the diplomatic door remains open, and a second round of talks could take place within days. Regional countries are negotiating with the United States to secure an extension of the fragile two-week ceasefire. Another key factor weighing on the economy comes from monetary policy. The minutes of the Federal Reserve's March meeting showed that despite the high degree of uncertainty brought about by the Iranian conflict, policymakers maintained their expectation of only one interest rate cut in 2026. On April 10th, CPI data was released. The US March unadjusted CPI annual rate was 3.3%, a new high since May 2024, in line with market expectations of 3.3%, compared to the previous value of 2.40%. The US March seasonally adjusted CPI monthly rate recorded 0.9%, a new high since June 2022, in line with market expectations. The US Bureau of Labor Statistics stated that the record rise in gasoline prices contributed nearly three-quarters of the CPI increase that month. Another indicator excluding food and energy costs saw its month-on-month increase slow to 0.2%. The market had initially hoped for a more aggressive easing cycle to boost risk assets, but the oil price shock made the Fed's "data-dependent" strategy more conservative. Higher energy costs could delay the decline in inflation, thus postponing interest rate cuts. This is undoubtedly a further blow to the highly liquidity-dependent crypto market. Historically, the failure of interest rate cut expectations has often been accompanied by a valuation reset of risk assets. BTC's current consolidation around $71,000 is a reflection of this adjustment in macroeconomic expectations. Currently, the latest data from Polymarket shows that the market is betting only a 26% probability of the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates by 25 basis points this year, while the probability of keeping rates unchanged has risen to 44%. Tightening market liquidity casts a shadow over the crypto market's potential rebound. Profit-taking suppresses BTC price rebound. On-chain data metrics reveal the true intentions of crypto players. Glassnode data shows that any attempt to reach the $70,000 to $80,000 range faced insufficient liquidity and profit-taking pressure, thus limiting the rebound's magnitude. Another rebound above $70,000 was exhausted by over $20 million in profit-taking per hour. Perhaps some sellers believe this BTC rebound is merely temporary, not a reversal. Currently, with the BTC price at $70,800, the number of addresses in a loss-making state is approximately 13.5 million. This indicates that a significant portion of users on the network purchased Bitcoin at a price higher than the current spot price. Despite short-term pressure, many crypto industry veterans remain structurally optimistic in the long term. Strategy founder Michael Saylor stated that Bitcoin likely bottomed out around $60,000 in early February, at which point forced traders to sell were shaken out of the market. The bottom was determined more by the exhaustion of sellers than by valuation. He believes current selling pressure is limited, ETF inflows are absorbing daily supply, and continued demand is being generated by companies allocating treasury assets to Bitcoin. Michael Saylor predicts the catalyst for the next bull market will be the formation of a banking and digital credit system built on Bitcoin, which will transform Bitcoin from a non-interest-bearing asset into a capital market engine. BitMEX founder Arthur Hayes stated in late March, "There's a lot of wishful thinking about a positive future in the market right now. Personally, I hope the killing stops, but I won't buy risky assets here." Tom Lee wrote that there are increasing signs that the market has "bottomed out," although the overall market remains skeptical. If you remain skeptical, you might consider buying assets that outperformed during the Iraq War.

data.title: Fake Ledger Wallet App Bypasses Apple Mac App Store Checks and Drains Nearly 6 BTC From US Musician
data.description:
The promise of secure self-custody turned into a costly mistake for one musician, after a convincing fake app slipped through official channels and wiped out years of savings in seconds.

Fake Ledger App Drains Musician’s Bitcoin Holdings

Garrett Dutton, better known as G. Love, has lost nearly 6 Bitcoin after unknowingly installing a malicious app posing as the official Ledger Live software from Apple’s Mac App Store.

The stolen 5.9 BTC was valued at more than $420,000 at the time, representing what he described as a decade of savings intended for retirement.

Dutton explained that he had been setting up his Ledger wallet on a new computer when he searched for the app through the App Store.

The version he downloaded appeared legitimate and guided him through a familiar setup process before requesting his 24-word recovery phrase.

Once entered, the attackers immediately emptied his wallet.

He said,

“I lost 5.9 BTC all I had for ten years I worked on this f#ck be careful out there.”

Source: X

How Did The Scam Slip Through The App Store

The fraudulent app mimicked the official interface closely enough to pass as genuine, raising concerns about how such software made its way onto a trusted platform like Apple’s marketplace.

Ledger has previously warned that even official app stores can host malicious copies designed purely to steal funds, urging users to download its wallet software only through its official website.

This tactic is not new.

Similar fake Ledger apps have surfaced on other platforms in recent years, including Microsoft’s store, where users collectively lost hundreds of thousands of dollars before the listings were removed.

Scammers have expanded their methods beyond app stores as well, using phishing emails, deceptive advertisements, and even physical mail to trick users into revealing sensitive information.

Funds Traced To Exchange But Recovery Unlikely

Blockchain investigator ZachXBT tracked the stolen Bitcoin across multiple transactions, identifying links to deposit addresses associated with KuCoin.

He said the funds were routed through several addresses, possibly via an instant exchange, making recovery difficult.

Hi I traced out your 5.92 BTC stolen and it was all laundered via @kucoincom deposit addresses in the following transactions:

6f5c8eb6b01774626f33527e0cb03c0d1860447acacd6079e69bf41b459bcf1f
9ee1288f941b2c3775ebd125eefeebdc713aa160bf2cf9d18661fd07f84ce891…

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) April 12, 2026

When asked whether the exchange might intervene, ZachXBT said he did not expect any action.

He also criticised KuCoin’s compliance approach, suggesting it responds selectively depending on circumstances.

The investigator pointed to the exchange’s loss of its MiCA licence in February 2026, only months after receiving it, as a sign of broader regulatory concerns.

He added that illicit actors continue to exploit broker and personal accounts on the platform with limited visible resistance.

Kucoin will not do anything they pretend to be compliant only when it benefits them.

Kucoin lost its MICA license in Feb 2026 after only having it for 3 months for a good reason. https://t.co/S7pMETtSwSpic.twitter.com/NK7SPBxAWI

— ZachXBT (@zachxbt) April 12, 2026

Security Experts Warn Against Sharing Seed Phrases Online

Security professionals have reiterated a long-standing rule in crypto: never enter a hardware wallet seed phrase on any internet-connected device.

Beau, head of security at Pudgy Penguins, warned that attackers rely on urgency and familiarity to trick users into giving up their recovery phrases.

He advised treating any prompt to download or update wallet software with suspicion unless verified independently.

Once a seed phrase is exposed, control of the wallet is effectively handed over, leaving little chance of reversing the loss.

This scam tactic tricks more people than it should

You will NEVER need to enter your hardware wallet seedphrase on an internet connected device (laptop, phone, smart fridge etc)

If you’re restoring a wallet, always do so by entering your seedphrase on a hardware wallet device… https://t.co/IYUV36Pnfa

— Beau (@beausecurity) April 11, 2026

Crypto Fraud Losses Continue To Climb

The incident reflects a wider surge in crypto-related fraud.

Recent FBI data shows that losses in the United States have climbed to $11.36 billion in 2025, alongside more than 181,000 reported complaints.

Crypto Fraud Losses Hit Record High

Americans lost a record $11.36 billion to crypto-related fraud in 2025, a 22% increase YoY.

➡️ Rising adoption is attracting more scams.
➡️ Fraud tactics becoming increasingly sophisticated.
➡️ Regulatory and enforcement pressure likely to… pic.twitter.com/VgHXmAyrYf

— 🌍kwutex Johnson (@De_Okwutex) April 11, 2026

Personal wallet theft alone accounted for hundreds of millions in losses, with many victims losing life savings to increasingly sophisticated scams.

Despite growing awareness, attackers continue to exploit trust in familiar platforms and interfaces, making even experienced users vulnerable—as Dutton himself admitted.

“I been in the crypto circus since 2017. Today they caught me off guard.”

data.title: American Singer G. Love Loses $420K Bitcoin Retirement Fund After Downloading Fake Ledger App From Apple Store
data.description:
American musician Garrett Dutton, better known as G. Love, has shared the story of how he has lost his retirement funds (estimated worth of $420,000) after falling victim to a malicious app scam

Philadelphia Musician G. Love Loses Nearly 6 BTC to Fake Ledger Wallet App on Apple's App Storehttps://t.co/C64ZYaotbN

— Bitcoin.com News (@BitcoinNews) April 12, 2026

Dutton shared how he watched the 5.9 BTC holdings which he had accumulated for nearly a decade disappear right from his eyes as soon as he entered his seed phrase on the malicious application that he downloaded from the Apple app store.

I had a really tough day today I lost my retirement fund in a hack/Scam when I switched my @Ledger over to my new computer and by accident downloaded a malicious ledger app from the @Apple store. All my BTC gone in an instant.

— G. Love (@glove) April 11, 2026

While Dutton regarded himself a veteran after spending almost 10 years in what he describes as the "crypto circus", he admitted the attack did catch him off guard.

"It was my own damn fault for not being more diligent. But let it serve as a warning. There's so many scams."

Blockchain Trail Leads To KuCoin

In a follow-up post, crypto sleuth investigator ZachXBT later revealed that the stolen funds were rerouted across nine transactions to deposit addresses associated with KuCoin.

Fake Ledger app on Apple’s Mac App Store just wiped out a musician’s entire 5.92 $BTC retirement in minutes . . . 🫣

The victim was American musician G Love.@zachxbt said the stolen BTC was routed through KuCoin deposit addresses.

🔒 Critical Safety Rules:

▸Never download… pic.twitter.com/ZioWq2o0Q7

— BeInCrypto (@beincrypto) April 13, 2026

While the exchange responded publicly, there has been no indication that the funds were recovered.

The case adds to a growing trend of crypto-related crime. According to the FBI, U.S. losses tied to crypto scams surpassed $11 billion in 2025, reflecting a sharp rise in fraudulent activity across the sector.

The FBI just confirmed that 2025 was the most expensive year in history for crypto scams. Nobody is talking about how the average loss per person skyrocketed because AI deepfakes are now perfectly mimicking trusted voices. Digital assets are being drained by sophisticated… https://t.co/2ECJ9YlamF

— KingZephyr (@KingZahyd) April 7, 2026

The scam relied on a malicious clone of Ledger Live, designed to trick users into entering their seed phrase—the master key that grants full access to a crypto wallet. Once exposed, funds can be transferred instantly and irreversibly.

Although the fake app was no longer visible on Apple’s App Store at the time of reporting, similar incidents have occurred before. In 2023, attackers used a fake Ledger app on Microsoft’s store to steal nearly $600,000 in Bitcoin, after bypassing platform review systems.

A Stark Reminder For Crypto Users

Dutton’s experience underscores a critical reality in crypto: self-custody offers full control—but also full responsibility.

Even with hardware wallets and established security practices, a single mistake—such as entering a seed phrase into an unverified app—can result in total loss.

$BTC is still trading around the $70,500 level.

It is increasingly unlikely that we will retest the $73K level, with price action suggesting further downside from here.

A break of $70,500 will result in significantly more downside, so this is an important level to watch! https://t.co/p8cVabXp6jpic.twitter.com/hJL7PnXZy9

— Wealthmanager (@Wealthmanager) April 13, 2026

His message to followers was simple but urgent: stay vigilant.

As crypto adoption grows, scams are becoming more sophisticated—targeting not just newcomers, but also long-time holders with significant assets.

Dutton’s loss is more than an isolated incident. It reflects a broader challenge for the industry: making self-custody both secure and user-friendly, without exposing users to devastating risks.

data.title: How AI, inflation, and scarcity are driving Bitcoin into its strongest bull run.
data.description:
Author: Jordi Visser, Senior Analyst on Wall Street; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

Some significant things are happening right now, and I believe this marks the beginning of the next phase of Bitcoin's market movement. For years, one of the biggest questions investors have had is simple: Bitcoin behaves like a risk asset. It fluctuates with the tech sector, and rises and falls with the software industry, market liquidity, and speculative sentiment. As long as this holds true, many investors prefer to stay within the fiat currency system and hold large-cap tech stocks. They don't need Bitcoin because the best-performing assets are still built on code, tied to traditional markets, and exist within the existing financial system.

This correlation is beginning to change. For most of the past three years, Bitcoin has been highly correlated with the software industry and has had to compete with short-term yields that outpace inflation. In this environment, investors could earn real returns by holding cash while also holding core growth stocks in the technology sector. Bitcoin could only struggle to gain attention. Even during its upward phase, many treated it as a secondary asset, not a core macro asset. Now, this格局 (structure/pattern) is crumbling. Bitcoin is beginning to decouple from the software sector, which itself is facing increasing pressure from the exponential growth of artificial intelligence (AI). This is crucial because it completely transforms Bitcoin's positioning in the minds of investors: Bitcoin is the only mainstream growth asset built on code that can benefit from, rather than be threatened by, the development of AI. This shift is key to the entire core logic. The software industry is facing a truly disruptive cycle. The Mythos model shifted the market discourse, forcing investors to consider: what happens when AI models move beyond incremental optimization and achieve leaps in capabilities? The industry challenges extend beyond software profit margins or corporate budgets, encompassing labor, knowledge-based jobs, cybersecurity risks, pricing power, and the sustainability of business models built for the previous generation of code. AI agents are accelerating this process, as they go beyond simply generating answers; they search, plan, negotiate, write code, and increasingly perform actions autonomously. This changes the pricing logic for software, the value of labor, and which parts of the traditional technology stack deserve a premium valuation. Bitcoin, however, is unaffected by this pressure because it is not a software company. It doesn't rely on user growth, pricing power, profit margins, or corporate spending. It is a digital scarcity asset. In a market reassessing all code-based assets, Bitcoin may be the only code-based asset that will become even stronger with the advancement of AI. This is a profound shift, as investors have viewed Bitcoin as an extension of the technology sector for years. What if the situation were exactly the opposite: that this digital asset actually benefits as exponential AI undermines other code-based economies? This is the first reason I believe we are entering a Bitcoin-exclusive cycle. The second reason lies on the macro level. A new wave of inflation appears imminent, while labor market momentum is weakening. This combination creates a policy dilemma for central banks. When inflation rises and the employment environment deteriorates, the Federal Reserve's room for aggressive inflation control shrinks significantly. It can only pause rate hikes, hesitate, and ultimately lean towards easing in an environment of continuously depreciating purchasing power. Historically, it is against this backdrop that Bitcoin has begun to move independently. The key signal is clear: the year-on-year CPI is about to exceed the 3-month Treasury yield. In other words, short-term real interest rates are about to turn negative again. Historically, this dividing line has been an important indicator of Bitcoin's performance. When cash yields exceed inflation, investors can hold short-term instruments to maintain purchasing power, forcing Bitcoin to compete with real yields. When inflation surpasses short-term yields, the situation reverses rapidly: cash ceases to function as a true store of value, and the seemingly safest nominal asset begins to underperform inflation. At this point, funds begin seeking alternatives. Based on my frequently used analytical framework, Bitcoin's strongest bullish cycles occur when the year-on-year CPI is higher than the 3-month Treasury yield, and the Federal Reserve pauses rate hikes or shifts towards easing. Within this range, Bitcoin's annualized return has exceeded 200%. We are now very close to this window. This is not a trivial macroeconomic detail, but rather the most favorable macroeconomic environment Bitcoin could experience.

What is particularly crucial now is that the reasons for this round of inflationary resurgence are completely different from the previous cycle. This is not a simple economic rebound, nor is it a temporary supply and demand imbalance in commodities. The root cause of rising inflation lies in the increasingly unstable demand for physical resources in the real economy, while artificial intelligence has significantly increased the demand for real-world production factors. Energy, electricity, computing power cooling, semiconductors, memory chips, power grid equipment, industrial metals, transportation, packaging, and logistics have all become core drivers of growth. Artificial intelligence should have been a story about software, but it has now evolved into a story about scarcity. The higher the level of intelligence integrated into the economic system, the greater the physical production capacity required to support it. This means that technologies once considered by investors to only lead to oversupply are now exacerbating inflationary pressures. The wave of creative destruction has been evolving in this direction for decades. Each wave of innovation makes the system more efficient, more deflationary, and more unequal by making capital returns superior to labor returns. This is also a major reason for the current global wealth distribution problem. Whenever deflation and unemployment threaten the economic system, central banks respond in a consistent manner: **cutting interest rates, releasing liquidity, launching quantitative easing, and stimulating demand in an attempt to offset the deflationary effects of technological change**. This approach has been effective in previous cycles, but it will fail against artificial intelligence. The power of this wave of technology far exceeds that of the past. People's purchasing power is already stretched thin, and policymakers now face a contradiction that is not easily resolved. While artificial intelligence (AI) creates deflationary pressures in the labor, software, and knowledge work sectors, it simultaneously drives up inflation in the real economy through electricity, commodities, cooling equipment, chips, and infrastructure. In other words, the same force is both depressing wages and jobs and raising the global cost structure necessary for its own development. Central banks can print money, but they cannot print copper, electricity, fertilizer, or stable purchasing power. These can support markets, but they cannot prevent exponential intelligence from disrupting the labor market. This is the unique aspect of this policy dilemma. Technological deflation and scarcity inflation are arriving simultaneously, and the traditional toolbox of quantitative easing is no longer suitable for this new world. And remember, humanoid robots will soon enter our lives; everything we are seeing now is just the beginning. With the emergence of the Mythos model, this logic has an increasingly important layer of meaning: the inherent fragility of the fiat currency system. For years, critics have viewed quantum computers as a threat to the future of Bitcoin. The current pressure stems from a more pressing direction. Mythos has drawn market attention to the risks of hacking and cybersecurity vulnerabilities within the existing financial architecture. The U.S. Treasury Department urgently convened a meeting of bank executives, and Anthropic spearheaded the Glasswing program to address the risks faced by businesses. These are significant moves, demonstrating that exponential AI is forcing institutions to confront the potentially enormous exposure risks within the financial system. If banking systems, payment channels, software architecture, and core digital infrastructure become increasingly vulnerable in an era of rapid AI model iteration, the security of the fiat currency system will fall far short of investor expectations. This represents a major shift in perception. Bitcoin was designed for a world where centralized systems have collapsed. The more artificial intelligence exposes the vulnerabilities of these systems, the more prominent Bitcoin's value becomes. Discussions about security are shifting from abstract future risks to current systemic vulnerabilities in institutions. This is what I have repeatedly emphasized—Bitcoin is the purest form of artificial intelligence trading instrument. This might sound counterintuitive, as Bitcoin is often discussed separately from semiconductors, data centers, robotics, or cloud computing infrastructure. However, the deeper connection lies in macroeconomic logic. **Artificial intelligence is reshaping the economy around scarcity, instability, and exponential change, forcing large-scale physical investment, increasing commodity consumption, and boosting electricity demand, while simultaneously transforming the underlying software.** Bitcoin stands precisely at the intersection of these forces: it benefits from negative real interest rates, market distrust of the fiat currency system, the fact that scarcity is more valuable than oversupply, and a world where nominal security is rapidly diverging from real purchasing power. There is also an important dimension underestimated by investors: **network effects driven by stablecoins.** Bitcoin already possesses one of the most powerful networks in the financial sector, is the most widely accepted store of value in the digital economy, and boasts the deepest liquidity, the broadest global recognition, the strongest institutional acceptance, and the most secure decentralized currency network. AI agents will further strengthen this advantage. When intelligent agents begin to transact, settle value, allocate capital, procure services, and interact with each other across platforms at machine speed, they will prioritize assets with the deepest liquidity, clearest monetary rules, highest operational stability, and widest acceptance. Network effects are continuously amplified through trust, scale, and interoperability, and Bitcoin possesses unique advantages in all three aspects. In a world where intelligent agents and humans participate in economic activities together, the Bitcoin network will become even more powerful because it provides a neutral, global, and highly liquid value channel that is acceptable to both humans and machines. Most importantly, AI intelligent agents will not be bound by the path dependence of the traditional fiat currency system; they will make choices based on optimal decision-making, which will ultimately benefit Bitcoin to the greatest extent. All of this is important because the rise of AI intelligent agents may create a completely new dimension of economic activity. We are moving towards a world where software no longer merely recommends or analyzes, but actively executes. Intelligent agents will represent users and businesses in searching, negotiating, procuring, settling, and optimizing. In such an economic system, a digital asset with a fixed supply, global portability, deep liquidity, and continuously improving institutional infrastructure will see its value continuously increase. Every new participant strengthens the network; every new wallet, institution, treasury, payment integration, custody platform, and settlement layer makes Bitcoin more useful to the next user. If AI agents become the primary initiators of future transactions, Bitcoin's network effect may become one of its most powerful long-term advantages. The credit aspect adds another layer of support to this logic. As artificial intelligence accelerates its disruption of the software industry, its impact will spread from public software service company valuations to private equity valuations, private credit ledgers, software-secured loans, and the broader financial environment. Investors intuitively understand this: when confidence in code-driven growth assets wanes, funds shift to scarce assets. Market trends have already confirmed this. The risks posed by AI disruption are driving investors to energy, metals, semiconductors, infrastructure, and increasingly, Bitcoin. There is only one growth asset globally that can exist both in the digital world and benefit from the transformation of the code economy by artificial intelligence—that is Bitcoin. Furthermore, there are global implications. The conflict in Iran has increased the probability of rising global food and energy prices, especially in emerging markets that rely on stable production costs. Developed markets can absorb some of the pressure through policy flexibility, reserve currency status, and deeper capital markets, while weaker emerging markets tend to be pressured more quickly. When the costs of food, diesel, fertilizer, and imported energy rise, local currencies will come under pressure, and purchasing power will depreciate rapidly. In this environment, Bitcoin will play a completely new role: it will no longer be just a speculative asset or portfolio diversification tool, but a safe-haven exit for currencies. When local currencies depreciate rapidly, and when households or investors want an asset that won't be diluted by domestic policies, Bitcoin becomes a safe haven for funds. If a new wave of inflation sweeps the globe, Bitcoin's importance will also increase accordingly. Technical analysis is now also beginning to resonate with macroeconomic logic. The weekly MACD indicator for Bitcoin has just formed a golden cross. The significance of such a technical signal is most profound when it aligns with a major underlying cyclical turning point. This is precisely the key moment: a continuously improving technical picture, real interest rates about to turn negative, Bitcoin's performance decoupling from the software sector, artificial intelligence exposing the fragility of fiat currency linkage systems, and the real demand from the new economy pushing up inflationary pressures—this is an extremely rare convergence. Moreover, even with these multiple positive factors, market sentiment in the crypto market and for Bitcoin remains extremely low, meaning that Bitcoin may not just be experiencing a rebound, but rather officially entering its own independent market cycle. This is precisely the situation that investors have been overlooking. They have always expected Bitcoin to follow the trend of traditional risk assets, but its next round of growth stems precisely from a complete shift in pricing logic. It is becoming the only code-based growth asset that benefits from artificial intelligence rather than being disrupted by it; it is becoming a core asset suited to a world of high inflation, weak employment, heightened scarcity, increased hacking risks, negative real interest rates, and fragile trust in the traditional financial system. The core logic lies here: Bitcoin doesn't need new stories. What it needs is for the world to enter the environment it was created to fit into. And all of this may already be happening.

data.title: The clear legislation is entering a critical phase and may influence the development of the crypto industry.
data.description: The progress of the Clarity Act in the United States has accelerated significantly, with legislators, regulators, and the crypto industry reaching a rare consensus to push the bill to the Senate Banking Committee for consideration. However, the controversy surrounding stablecoin yields remains a core obstacle, and with the November midterm elections approaching, the legislative window is in its final countdown. The sense of urgency for the bill's passage is growing, with Senator Cynthia Lummis explicitly stating that this is the "last chance" to pass the bill before 2030, warning that missing this opportunity would impact the future of American finance. Former White House crypto director David Sacks has also called on the Senate to pass the bill, believing it can continue the achievements of the Genius Act and establish America's leadership in the digital asset field. With the midterm elections approaching, congressional priorities may shift, potentially delaying the crypto legislation process and further highlighting the urgency of advancing the bill. Currently, multiple forces have formed a combined impetus to drive this development. On the regulatory front, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent published an article in the Wall Street Journal urging accelerated legislation, pointing out that a lack of clear regulation would cause the US to lag behind jurisdictions like Abu Dhabi and Singapore, and emphasizing that enduring laws are key to attracting blockchain developers and entrepreneurs back to the US. SEC Chairman Paul Atkins also expressed support, believing the bill could prevent "rogue regulators" and should be submitted to the president for signature as soon as possible. At the industry level, Chris Dixon, managing partner of a16z Crypto, and Robbie Ferguson, founder of Immutable, both stated that clear regulation would benefit consumers and entrepreneurs, and could even drive the industry to surpass the growth of the gaming industry over the past decade. Coinbase's shift in stance has been a significant driving force. Its CEO, Brian Armstrong, who had previously twice refused to support the bill, believing the draft version was inferior to the current regulatory landscape, has now responded to Bessent's call and publicly expressed his support for its advancement. Its Chief Legal Officer, Paul Grewal, pointed out that the key to the bill's successful passage lies in whether all parties can reach a consensus on the stablecoin yield controversy, which remains the core obstacle to its progress. It is understood that the Clarity Act aims to clarify the regulatory rules for crypto assets and delineate the jurisdiction of the SEC and CFTC. It complements the Genius Act previously signed by Trump, whose implementation, while proving the feasibility of crypto legislation, requires the support of the Clarity Act for full implementation. This bill has become a litmus test for Washington's determination to regulate crypto. Although various parties have formed a united front, unresolved stablecoin controversies and the tightening election window still bring uncertainty. Whether it can ultimately pass will directly determine the United States' competitiveness in the global digital finance field.

data.title: Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses: TradFi gets the key first, Web3 is still on the sidelines.
data.description:
Author:Wu Shuo Blockchain

On April 10, 2026, Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation entered the "operational" stage: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority announced that, in accordance with the Stablecoin Ordinance, it granted a stablecoin issuer license to Anchorpoint Financial Technology Co., Ltd., a joint venture established by HSBC, Standard Chartered Bank, Anchorpoint Group, and Hong Kong Telecom. The license took effect immediately, and the two institutions will commence operations in the coming months after completing the necessary preparations. Affected by this news, related Hong Kong stocks all rose, with Guotai Junan International seeing a maximum increase of 27.69%, and Yunfeng Financial (a stablecoin concept stock) rising by 8.74%.

data.title: Trump-Backed WLFI Token Hits Record Low After $75M Self-Collateralized Loan Revealed
data.description:
World Liberty Financial’s WLFI token—linked to Donald Trump—fell to an all-time low of $0.07714 after the project was caught using billions of its own tokens as collateral to secure $75 million in stablecoin loans, raising fresh concerns about liquidity risk and DeFi market stability.

WLFI Leverage Loop Near Limit, Dominates Dolomite Borrowing

Chaos Labs reported that World Liberty Finance (WLFI) has rapidly expanded borrowing on Dolomite, pushing collateral use near its 5.1B cap across two multisigs; ~3B WLFI backs ~$40.7M in stables (mainly USD1), with a… pic.twitter.com/TeeaSDLaIU

— Wu Blockchain (@WuBlockchain) April 13, 2026

83% drop from peak rattles market confidence

Since the news break, the price of WLFI has now plunged 83% from its $0.46 September high, recently trading around $0.07879.

$wlfi weekly is about to fall off a cliff …. Towards $0.064 next …

Could be a bloody few weeks especially combined with “sale in May and go away”

Nfa !!!#wlfi#crypto#altcoins$btchttps://t.co/KN2XdOt6Wppic.twitter.com/el7w1iyCOs

— Pepe 💎FUND Crypto Moon Boi💎 (@XYOPepe) April 12, 2026

Onchain data shows wallets tied to World Liberty Financial deposited roughly 5 billion WLFI tokens on Dolomite—a protocol co-founded by the project’s CTO, Corey Caplan—to borrow $75 million in stablecoins, including USDC.

More than $40 million was subsequently transferred to Coinbase Prime, adding to speculation about how the borrowed funds are being deployed.

Analysts warn that despite a fully diluted valuation (FDV) near $10 billion, WLFI remains a relatively illiquid asset—meaning large sell-offs could significantly impact price. In a downside scenario, this raises the risk of forced liquidations that could cascade through the lending position.

The structure quickly drew criticism across Crypto X, with some users comparing the model to “printing casino chips and borrowing real money against them.”

Critics argue that using a project’s own token as collateral introduces reflexive risk—where falling prices weaken collateral, potentially triggering liquidations that further accelerate the decline.

In case you're wondering why Dolomite allows this:

Corey Caplan, the co-founder of Dolomite, is also the CTO of WorldLibertyFi!

So, as he has a financial incentive here, Dolomite is open to throwing its lenders under the bus.

The solution to this issue is simple:

Set a… pic.twitter.com/rjGqQBWz5T

— Ethan DeFi (@EthanDeFi_) April 8, 2026

The concern is amplified by the relatively small size of Dolomite, which ranks outside the top tier of DeFi lending platforms by total value locked. A large, concentrated position could therefore have outsized impact on the protocol if unwound abruptly.

Blockchain analytics from Arkham Intelligence helped surface the wallet activity, intensifying scrutiny around the structure and governance of the project.

World Liberty defends strategy as yield play

World Liberty Financial pushed back on the criticism, stating that its positions remain “well above liquidation thresholds.”

The team described itself as an “anchor borrower,” arguing that the strategy is designed to generate enhanced stablecoin yields for users in a low-yield environment.

“Everyday users are earning outsized stablecoin yields right now—at a time when traditional markets are offering very little.”

Here's what the FUD crowd is missing entirely:
By being the anchor borrower, we're generating the yield that makes WLFI Markets compelling for everyone else.
Everyday users are earning outsized stablecoin yields right now — at a time when traditional markets are offering very…

— WLFI (@worldlibertyfi) April 9, 2026

Separately, the team signaled plans for a governance proposal that would introduce a phased token unlock schedule for early investors, replacing immediate liquidity with longer-term vesting in an effort to stabilize supply.

A stress test for DeFi risk management

The WLFI episode highlights a broader tension in DeFi: how protocols handle large, self-referential collateral positions.

While such strategies can enhance capital efficiency, they also introduce structural fragility—particularly when liquidity is thin and positions are concentrated.

With Dolomite’s relatively modest footprint, any sharp move in WLFI’s price could test the resilience of both the lending platform and its participants.

For now, the project maintains that its strategy is sustainable. But as volatility persists, WLFI’s trajectory may become a key case study in how far DeFi can stretch before risk mechanics begin to break down.

data.title: a16z: The Operating Principles and Logic Behind the Explosive Growth of Perpetual Contracts
data.description:
Author: Jay Drain Jr., Partner at a16z crypto; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

Perpetual contracts (perps) are futures contracts with no expiration date. Originally an innovation in the crypto space, this type of product experienced explosive growth on-chain in 2025. Today, it has become one of the largest markets in the crypto industry, with trading instruments covering traditional assets and trading volumes reaching trillions of dollars.

Last year, the total liquidation volume of perpetual contracts on leading centralized exchanges (CEXs) reached $86.2 trillion (a year-on-year increase of 47%), while the growth rate of on-chain perpetual contracts was even more rapid: the trading volume of mainstream decentralized exchanges (DEXs) reached $6.7 trillion (a year-on-year increase of 346%). Currently, the trading volume of decentralized exchanges is approximately 7.8% of that of centralized exchanges, compared to only about 2.5% a year ago. (Note: Although a few centralized platforms regulated in the US offer products similar to perpetual contracts to US users, all centralized and decentralized exchanges restrict US users from participating in genuine perpetual contract trading.) But a more important trend is that perpetual contracts are gradually shedding their label as niche crypto-native products, becoming a fundamental transformation in trading behavior and market structure. So, what exactly is driving the popularity of perpetual contracts? And why now? This article will analyze why perpetual contracts are increasingly favored by global traders, the scale of the market opportunity, and the opportunities seen by developers. A Brief History and Evolution of Perpetual Contracts The concept of perpetual contracts actually predates the crypto industry. As early as 1993, Nobel laureate economist Robert Shiller proposed perpetual futures contracts, initially envisioning them as a tool for hedging real estate value risk. However, it wasn't until 2016, with the rise of BitMEX and XBTUSD, the longest-running Bitcoin perpetual swap, that perpetual contracts truly gained popularity in the crypto space. Ten years later, major exchanges have launched perpetual futures contracts covering stocks, stock indices, commodities, interest rates, startup valuations, and even the price of Nvidia H100 GPUs. For years, perpetual contracts have been a billion-dollar revenue engine for centralized exchanges. With the growing demand for leveraged trading from retail investors, perpetual contracts have become a primary venue for short-term price discovery, liquidity aggregation, and trading activity—on several leading centralized exchanges (CEXs) in Asia, perpetual contract trading volume is several times that of spot trading volume. A key change over the past year and a half has been the substantial erosion of centralized exchanges' market share in the perpetual contract market by decentralized perpetual contract exchanges (Perp DEXs). Leveraging the structural advantage of self-custody, decentralized perpetual contract platforms are rapidly closing the gap with centralized exchanges in terms of liquidity, performance, and functionality to meet the needs of active traders. With the explosive success of decentralized perpetual contract platforms like Hyperliquid, leading crypto wallets and applications have integrated perpetual contract products, creating superior trading experiences that are easily accessible to millions of users. In the second half of 2025, decentralized perpetual contract front-end applications experienced explosive growth—from simple mobile applications to professional multi-platform trading terminals emerging one after another. Hyperliquid, in particular, broke through the limitations of decentralized exchanges through its HIP-3 (Developer Deployment of Perpetual Contracts) mechanism, which allows anyone to list perpetual contracts on the platform without permission. With HIP-3, developers can list almost any asset, receive a 50% commission, and independently manage oracles and risk parameters. Meanwhile, emerging platforms such as Avantis, Lighter, Ostium, and Variational also appeared or accelerated their product development. Increasingly fierce competition forced decentralized perpetual contract exchanges to seek differentiation in architectural design, market structure, asset support, and permissionless features, and also enabled some trading platforms to achieve efficient product-market matching in new tracks such as Real-World Asset (RWA) perpetual contracts. For years, perpetual contract traders were limited to speculating on crypto assets—Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, and numerous long-tail altcoins. However, in the latter half of last year, despite a significant drop in perpetual contract trading volume from recent highs amid a general crypto market sell-off, Real-World Asset (RWA) perpetual contracts bucked the trend and surged. Multiple decentralized perpetual contract platforms launched commodity, stock, and stock index contracts, significantly expanding the range of tradable assets, from unlisted companies like Nvidia and Samsung to commodities such as silver and palladium. This year, the growth of RWA perpetual contracts has accelerated further. In recent weeks, RWA-related trading volume has accounted for as much as 44% of Hyperliquid's total trading volume, and RWA trading pairs have consistently been among the highest-contributing trading pairs in terms of platform fees. On the Ostium platform, RWA trading volume has accounted for the vast majority of the platform's total trading volume for several consecutive months.

Decentralized exchanges also excel in price discovery for real-world assets (RWAs) such as crude oil, especially during weekends when traditional exchanges are closed. With the explosion of perpetual contracts for real-world assets, we've seen a surge of companies developing products and services related to perpetual contracts. In just the past six months, new exchanges, trading interfaces, market deployment tools, and liquidity providers have emerged. Players entering this space include entirely new startups, ventures moving into the perpetual contract arena, and some of the world's largest fintech companies—integrating perpetual contract trading into their existing products. All these players are targeting the same opportunity: perpetual contracts are poised to become one of the mainstream trading tools in the global financial landscape. Market Opportunities for Perpetual Contracts Looking at traditional financial markets, options are one of the world's largest and most actively traded markets. Options cover a wide range of instruments, including currencies, stocks, stock indices, commodities, and ETFs. They are powerful and flexible tools that allow for trading based on various judgments, such as timing, volatility, and price ranges. However, focusing on retail investor behavior reveals a significant concentration of trading on a specific type of option: short-term, leveraged, and directional exposure. A typical example is 0DTE (On-Demand Expiry Options), where traders buy low-cost convex exposures to capture intraday price fluctuations. This type of trading is one of the fastest-growing segments within the options market. In 2025, the average daily trading volume of S&P 500 0DTE options reached 2.3 million contracts, a year-on-year increase of 51%, accounting for 59% of the total S&P 500 options trading volume. To meet this demand, the market has launched several new daily expiring index products, including CBTX and MBTX Bitcoin ETF index options, as well as equal-weight index options for the Chicago Board Options Exchange's "Top Ten Giants." Therefore, despite the many complex uses of options—structured hedging, volatility trading, dispersion trading, convexity strategies, etc.—the massive and continuously growing retail investor funds are actually only seeking short-term, leveraged directional exposure. This is precisely the kind of demand that perpetual contracts best meet. The trade-off between the two is clear: **options excel at fixed risk and convex returns, remaining the default tool for expressing volatility views, with traders' maximum loss limited to the premium paid.** Perpetual contracts, on the other hand, could potentially lead to the liquidation of all collateralized assets. However, for most retail investors who truly desire leveraged directional trading, perpetual contracts offer several structural advantages:

  1. 24/7 Trading. The next-generation perpetual contract market operates 24/7, with no trading hours restrictions or interruptions. For global, crypto-native users, uninterrupted trading is a basic expectation.

  2. No Strike Price, No Expiry Date, No Rollover. Single, continuous position holding eliminates the need for traders to select parameters, manage expiry dates, or rebuild positions daily or weekly. Positions can be held from seconds to months, and theoretically, indefinitely.

  3. Simpler Risk Dimensions. The core considerations for perpetual contracts are only price, margin, and liquidation threshold. Even if the direction of an option trade is correctly predicted, losses can still occur due to time decay, changes in implied volatility, and path dependence. Perpetual contracts eliminate these complexities, making trading purely about directional judgment. Longer exposures are more capital-efficient. Short-term options require full premium payment and frequent rollovers. Perpetual contracts only require margin—usually a small fraction of the notional value—making them generally more capital-efficient for intraday to multi-day directional positions. Options are not disappearing. They are already part of financial history and are likely to continue to dominate in many trading scenarios, especially those requiring fixed risk and more complex payout structures. But for the massive and ever-growing flow of funds seeking delta-linked, directional leverage, perpetual contracts have already captured trillions of dollars in trading volume and billions of dollars in revenue. This raises the question: As perpetual contracts evolve from niche tools to mainstream financial infrastructure components, at what level of the industry chain will their value be realized? In traditional markets, the most valuable companies are often built on top of exchange infrastructure, not the exchanges themselves. For example, the market capitalization of retail brokerage Robinhood is higher than that of its parent company, Nasdaq. Whether this pattern holds true in the crypto space—whether platforms like Hyperliquid, Lighter, and Ostium can accumulate sufficiently strong network effects at the exchange level—remains one of the most noteworthy points of contention in the industry. Regardless of the outcome, the developer ecosystem is expanding rapidly. We've seen developer growth concentrated in several areas: **Differentiated Distribution Layer:** Front-end products targeting vertical markets or specific demographics, not just displaying market data, but also integrating narrative, strategy, gamification, or social elements. **Marketplace Creation and Operation (e.g., HIP-3 Deployers):** Operating a popular marketplace on Hyperliquid is equivalent to owning a mini-exchange without needing to build the most complex underlying trading system. The data and price sources that today's deployers access may only be the tip of the iceberg in the "perpetual contract" paradigm. **Specialized Liquidity Provision:** Market makers specializing in long-tail markets, event-driven order books, and cross-platform position management. Dedicated Data Infrastructure for Perpetual Contracts: A community-driven ecosystem of dashboards, block explorers, heatmaps, and analytics tools has been initially established, encompassing dimensions such as open positions, funding rates, liquidation, trader signals, leverage exposure, and user retention. More mature and high-quality real-time data will make the entire ecosystem more transparent and efficient for all participants. Of course, the industry still faces many unresolved issues and challenges, including channel distribution, liquidity depth on emerging platforms, oracle reliability with the expansion of asset range, extreme edge scenarios such as "10/10," and regulatory aspects—currently, US users are restricted from using such products. As perpetual contracts move beyond the crypto-native realm and onto the global financial stage, these are all foreseeable growing pains. As the perpetual contract ecosystem matures, the question is no longer whether perpetual contracts can be scaled, but rather who can build the most valuable applications and infrastructure around them in the process.

data.title: Chinese institutions cool down their enthusiasm for Hong Kong stablecoins
data.description:
Author: Xie Zhaoqing, Tencent News "Deep Dive"

After more than a year of stablecoin sandbox testing and multiple rounds of twists and turns, Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses has finally been issued.

On the afternoon of April 10, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority announced the first batch of companies that have obtained stablecoin licenses. Contrary to previous market speculation that companies with Chinese capital backgrounds such as Ant Group and Guotai Junan International would be involved, the first batch of companies consists of only two: HSBC Hong Kong and IDC Financial Technology Co., Ltd.

IDC Financial Technology Co., Ltd.'s shareholders are Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Animoca Brands, and HKT (Hong Kong Telecom), owned by Richard Li.

The shareholders of IDC Financial Technology Co., Ltd. are Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Animoca Brands, and HKT (Hong Kong Telecom), owned by Richard Li.

Unlike HSBC's application, Dingdian Financial Technology Co., Ltd. is actually a joint venture between the three companies mentioned above, and its management team is independent of the three shareholder companies. This joint venture was one of the first three participants to enter the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's stablecoin "sandbox" test in July 2024, the other two being JD CoinChain and Yuanbi Technology. If USDT (a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar) is the "Wild West" of the digital world, then the stablecoin licenses issued by Hong Kong this time are the first concrete wall built for this wasteland, supported by regulatory rules and the legal financial system. This is indeed one of the few pieces of good news for those in the cryptocurrency market during a bear market. Several heads of cryptocurrency and traditional funds told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that the cryptocurrency market, lacking a narrative, is "as still as stagnant water," and stablecoins are the most important narrative for the future of the cryptocurrency market, marking a milestone for Hong Kong's financial sector. Hong Kong Monetary Authority Deputy Chief Executive Chan Wai-man stated at the event that compared to other jurisdictions, Hong Kong has consistently been at the forefront of the stablecoin business, including launching stablecoin sandbox testing, enacting the Stablecoin Ordinance, and formally issuing licenses. He believes that the issuance of stablecoin licenses is indeed a crucial step for the Hong Kong market. According to the plan, the two licensees will officially launch their stablecoins as early as the second half of this year. Before that, they must complete system testing and implement risk management measures. Chen Weimin has repeatedly emphasized that the timetable for issuing the second batch of licenses is yet to be determined, as it depends on the operational status of the first two applicants. Furthermore, the total number of licenses issued in the future will be very limited. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" has learned that all the Chinese-funded institutions that submitted applications received "window guidance" from relevant departments, requesting them to postpone their participation in this stablecoin license application process. Despite this, some Chinese-funded institutions have maintained communication with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority over the past few months. One person in charge of the license application stated that after excluding Chinese-funded institutions, the vast majority of prospective applicants have limited resources, and the number of qualified institutions that fully meet the requirements of the Stablecoin Ordinance is not large. However, to the surprise of outsiders, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority ultimately selected only two entities: HSBC and Standard Chartered. The regulatory screening process is more like "unpacking a blind box": Risk management capabilities and application scenarios are the core considerations. When asked how the first two approved companies were selected from 36 formal applicants, Chen Weimin briefly told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that, "Although 36 companies submitted applications, only these two met the HKMA's requirements." How were the first two companies selected? Chen Weimin replied simply, "The HKMA team went through a very complicated process before selecting only two that met the requirements." Regarding the application for Yuan Coins that were already in the sandbox, why the conditions were not met, Chen Weimin stated that he "cannot disclose" the details of other companies. The information states that "Comments are declined due to [reason], and it is emphasized that 'entering the sandbox does not guarantee that a company will obtain a license, and not entering the sandbox does not mean that a company is not [involved in the process of obtaining a license].'" "It is not possible to obtain a license." A source in Hong Kong's financial sector told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that compared to the previous issuance of digital bank licenses in Hong Kong, which selected some non-bank institutions, such as ZhongAn, this time stablecoins have not chosen any non-bank institutions. "Hong Kong regulators may now be more conservative." A manager at a cryptocurrency company was even more direct in his comments to Tencent News's "Deep Dive." The text indicates that, aside from the prohibition on issuing licenses to Chinese companies, even Yuanbi (a local Hong Kong company) in the sandbox failed to obtain a license. This may reflect the current distrust of Hong Kong regulators towards innovation by small and medium-sized financial technology companies, leading them to prefer banks for innovative activities. "From the outside, this selection process might seem more like opening a blind box. Judging from the results, regulators may have been more concerned with the bank's background, ultimately selecting these two," a cryptocurrency industry insider analyzed to Tencent News's "Deep Dive." "However, many of the selection rules are indeed incomprehensible to outsiders." On the same day, Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) Chief Executive Eddie Yue stated that the HKMA primarily considers two aspects when approving licenses: whether the applicant possesses sufficient risk management capabilities and experience, and complies with relevant regulations in Hong Kong and other regions; and whether the applicant can propose specific application scenarios and feasible business solutions and development plans. As it stands now, the application scenarios of these two institutions are rather lackluster. Standard Chartered's RD Technologies and HSBC announced on the same day that they will currently only issue Hong Kong dollar-pegged stablecoins, focusing more on local Hong Kong applications. Compared to USD and renminbi stablecoins, the pool size and user base of Hong Kong dollar stablecoins are significantly smaller. In a competitive landscape where USD-pegged stablecoins already hold nearly 70% of the market share, these two licensed institutions have not yet provided clear incentive plans to encourage users to switch to Hong Kong dollar stablecoins. This is one of the important reasons why the issuance of Hong Kong stablecoin licenses has not generated a strong response in the cryptocurrency community. Chen Weimin stated at the event that if the two licensed institutions plan to issue new currency stablecoins, they will need to communicate separately with the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), such as USD stablecoins or renminbi stablecoins. The future business plans of these two licensees include cross-border payments, tokenized asset trading, and innovative applications such as conditional payments and supply chain financing—however, neither company has provided a specific timetable for the implementation of these businesses. HSBC's application scenarios include its Payme and HSBC App payment services. Payme has over 3.3 million users, including 160,000 merchants—more like a new stablecoin addition to HSBC's mobile payment system. The wallet. HSBC management revealed at the event that, in addition to their existing accounts, users will have a built-in mobile account under their HSBC mobile account. Stablecoin Account — This stablecoin account requires user application before activation. HSBC's Payme head, Li Guankang, told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that any Payme user can open a stablecoin account. Within the stablecoin account, users can directly transfer funds to friends and family, transfer funds to merchants, or invest in stablecoin-linked products. However, he also mentioned that users on the HSBC app cannot directly open a stablecoin account—they must wait until HSBC selects eligible stablecoin users before they can apply for a stablecoin account. This is because Payme account opening requires users to be Hong Kong residents, while HSBC app users include mainland users. Under the existing management framework in Hong Kong, only Hong Kong residents can participate in the trading of virtual assets—even if they are mainland customers. Hong Kong HSBC mobile payment users are also not eligible to apply for stablecoin accounts. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" has learned that HSBC had not yet decided whether to apply for a stablecoin license in Hong Kong until September 2025. Insiders indicated that HSBC remained concerned about the risks associated with stablecoins at the time. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority's (HKMA) application deadline was September 30, 2025—the exact date HSBC submitted its application is unknown. However, the HKMA had previously stated that applications could be submitted after September 30. Li Guankang told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that HSBC has previously participated in central bank digital currencies and tokenized deposits, and has been exploring new businesses. In fact, HSBC has been communicating with Hong Kong regulators regarding stablecoins. However, he did not disclose when HSBC would specifically participate in the stablecoin license application. The Road to Hong Kong Stablecoin Licenses Without Chinese Institutions: A Hidden Game The absence of Chinese institutions in Hong Kong's first batch of stablecoin licenses is not surprising. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority has stated that it has received applications from 77 interested institutions, including sandbox participants, banks, and payment giants. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" has learned that among the 77 applicants are dozens of Chinese-funded institutions. Several of these, including Lianlian Pay and gaming company NetEase, submitted their applications to the Hong Kong Monetary Authority before the application deadline of October 31, 2025. As of press time, Lianlian Pay and NetEase could not be reached for comment. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" exclusively learned that the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) and mainland regulators subsequently released the list of 77 institutions simultaneously. An informed source told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that since November 2025, many Chinese-funded institutions that had already submitted applications have received notices requiring them to temporarily refrain from participating in the application process for Hong Kong stablecoin licenses. The aforementioned source told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that this was all expected. In August 2025, before the Hong Kong Stablecoin Ordinance officially came into effect on August 1st of that year, a large number of Chinese-funded institutions suddenly flocked to Hong Kong, hoping to apply for licenses. At that time, the Hong Kong stablecoin license application process had already begun. Following the Hong Kong government's release of stablecoin application guidelines at the end of July 2025, according to the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's (HKMA) timetable, institutions intending to apply for licenses could formally establish connections with the HKMA and other institutions after August 1st, and formally submit application materials to the HKMA by September 30th. The final application deadline was September 30th. The peak period for mainland Chinese capital inflows into Hong Kong's Central district is expected to be around July and August 2025. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" learned that in early July alone, more than 20 mainland Chinese institutions met with the head of the HKMA's fintech department responsible for stablecoin matters. He Hongzhe, then head of the Digital Finance Department of the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) responsible for approving stablecoin licenses and related business in Hong Kong, was considered by these institutions to be the most difficult person to reach. These institutions include, but are not limited to, Chinese-funded financial institutions and state-owned enterprises in Hong Kong. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" exclusively learned that on August 6, 2025, Guo Guangchang, founder of Fosun Group, personally led key members of Fosun's stablecoin team and other senior executives to meet with John Lee, Chief Executive of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, and Paul Chan, Financial Secretary of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region, within a single day. The Financial Secretary is a direct superior agency of the HKMA, the regulatory body for approving stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" has learned that during a meeting with Hong Kong's Financial Secretary Paul Chan, a mainland entrepreneur was advised by Chan to apply for stablecoin licenses in conjunction with banking institutions, as these institutions have experience in payment and anti-money laundering. Several sources familiar with the matter told Tencent News's "Deep Dive" that Chan made similar suggestions to several institutions he met with. Subsequently, the vast majority of companies intending to apply for stablecoin licenses began flocking to Hong Kong banks, seeking cooperation in the application process. Partnering with banks to apply for stablecoin licenses became standard practice. This has also forced some institutions in the sandbox to start seeking cooperation with banks. Looking at the first batch of licenses issued by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA), HSBC and Standard Chartered's subsidiary, Idle Fintech, both come from large banks. As Chen Maobo suggested at the time, there was a greater emphasis on "banking background." The stablecoin craze was also spreading in mainland China at the time. Tencent News' "Deep Dive" learned that a first-tier city in mainland China once hoped to try stablecoin business in its free trade zone. However, this idea was quickly abandoned. The turning point occurred on October 27, 2025. At the 2025 Financial Street Forum Annual Meeting, Pan Gongsheng, Governor of the People's Bank of China, delivered a keynote speech entitled "The Construction Practice and Future Evolution of China's Macroprudential Management System," in which he mentioned that "as a financial activity, stablecoins currently cannot effectively meet basic requirements such as customer identification and anti-money laundering, amplifying loopholes in global financial regulation, such as money laundering, illegal cross-border fund transfers, and terrorist financing. The market is rife with speculation, increasing the vulnerability of the global financial system." He also mentioned that the People's Bank of China will work with law enforcement agencies to continue cracking down on the operation and speculation of virtual currencies within China to maintain economic and financial order, while closely monitoring and dynamically assessing the development of stablecoins overseas. This statement directly cooled the enthusiasm of Chinese-funded institutions in Hong Kong who were rushing to apply for stablecoin licenses at the time. Many even hesitated to submit their applications before the September 30, 2025 application deadline. Tencent News's "Deep Dive" learned that some of the bolder institutions actually did submit their applications and received routine inquiries and responses from the Hong Kong Monetary Authority in the following months. Most Chinese-funded institutions essentially halted their Hong Kong stablecoin license application process by early December. Public information shows that in early December 2025, the People's Bank of China led a meeting of the coordination mechanism for combating speculation in virtual currency trading, attended by 13 national-level regulatory departments including the Ministry of Public Security, the Cyberspace Administration of China, and the Central Financial Affairs Commission, formally including stablecoins within the scope of virtual currency regulation. This meeting clarified that stablecoins fall under the category of virtual currencies, and their related business activities are subject to the regulatory framework for illegal financial activities. Stablecoins fail to meet compliance requirements such as customer identification and anti-money laundering, posing a risk of being used for illegal activities such as money laundering, fundraising fraud, and illegal cross-border fund transfers, necessitating strengthened regulation across the entire supply chain. Several individuals who were in Hong Kong applying for stablecoin licenses at the time stated that this joint meeting sent a strong regulatory signal and was the most direct indication of a "cooling down" of the stablecoin market. Subsequently, Tencent News's "Deep Dive" learned that even Chinese-funded institutions that had officially submitted their applications had temporarily suspended their applications for stablecoin licenses in Hong Kong. However, these people are more optimistic. They believe that although Chinese-funded institutions were not included in the first batch of licenses in Hong Kong, as a pilot area, they will still be the main force in building the stablecoin ecosystem in Hong Kong in the future, and may even have the opportunity to participate in the application for stablecoin licenses in the future.

data.title: Stop focusing on GPUs; CPUs are becoming the "new bottleneck" in the AI ​​era.
data.description:
Author: Li Hailun, Tencent Technology

In the years of AI's rapid development, the industry has been almost dominated by one logic: computing power determines the upper limit, and GPUs are the core of computing power.

However, entering 2026, this logic began to change: model inference was no longer the only bottleneck; system performance increasingly depended on execution and scheduling capabilities. GPUs remained important, but the key to whether AI could "run" was gradually shifting to the long-neglected CPU. On April 9th, US time, Google and Intel reached a multi-year agreement to deploy Intel's Xeon processors on a global scale in AI data centers, precisely to overcome this bottleneck. Intel CEO Chen Liwu stated bluntly that for AI to run on the entire system, the CPU and IPU are the key to performance, efficiency, and flexibility. In other words, the CPU, which had been treated as a "supporting role" for the past two years, is now the "neck" restricting the expansion of AI. Intel CEO Chen Liwu stated on social media: Intel is deepening its cooperation with Google, expanding from traditional CPUs to AI infrastructure (such as IPUs), to jointly promote the development of AI and cloud computing capabilities. CPUs are no longer just passive supporting components, but are becoming one of the key variables in AI infrastructure. A Silent Supply Crisis While everyone is focused on GPU delivery times, the tension in the CPU market has quietly built up. According to the latest reports from multiple IT distributors, the average selling price of server CPUs rose by approximately 30% in the fourth quarter of 2025. This increase is very rare in a relatively mature CPU market. Forrest Norrod, head of AMD's data center division, revealed that CPU demand has grown faster than expected over the past three quarters. Currently, AMD's delivery cycle has extended from eight weeks to more than ten weeks, with some models facing delays of up to six months. This shortage is primarily due to a resource squeeze triggered by a "secondary effect." Industry insiders have stated that because TSMC's 3nm production lines are extremely strained, the wafer capacity originally allocated to CPUs is being increasingly squeezed by orders for the more profitable GPUs. This has led to a highly ironic situation: AI labs have enough GPUs, but find that they cannot buy enough top-tier CPUs on the market to power these graphics cards. Elon Musk is also involved in this round of CPU buying frenzy. Intel CEO Li-Wu Chen confirmed on social media that Musk has commissioned Intel to design and manufacture custom chips for his "Terafab" project in Texas. This massive project aims to provide a unified computing foundation for xAI, SpaceX, and Tesla. Musk's trust in Intel largely stems from Intel's attempt to embed itself into every level, from terrestrial data centers to orbital computing. For Intel, this is undoubtedly a shot in the arm. Industry analysts predict that AMD's revenue share in the server CPU market will surpass Intel's by 2026. However, Intel's deep-rooted presence in the x86 ecosystem and its manufacturing capabilities remain a significant asset that major customers like Musk cannot ignore. This deep cross-industry integration is transforming competition in the CPU market from a simple parameter comparison to a battle for ecosystem and supply chain stability. Why has the CPU become a "weak link"? The core reason the CPU has suddenly become a bottleneck is that its workload has fundamentally changed in the era of intelligent agents. In traditional chatbot models, the CPU is mainly responsible for scheduling and data processing, while the GPU handles the core inference computation. Because computationally intensive tasks are concentrated on the GPU side, overall latency is usually dominated by the GPU, and the CPU rarely becomes a performance bottleneck. However, the workload of an agent is completely different. An agent needs to perform multi-step reasoning, call APIs, read and write databases, orchestrate complex business flows, and integrate intermediate results into the final output. Tasks such as searching, API calls, code execution, file I/O, and result orchestration mostly fall on the CPU and host system side. The GPU is responsible for token generation (i.e., "thinking"), while the CPU is responsible for translating the "thinking" results into actual actions. In their paper "A CPU-Centric Perspective on Agentic AI," published in November 2025, Georgia Tech researchers conducted a quantitative analysis of the latency distribution in agent workloads. The study found that CPU-side tool processing accounts for 50% to 90.6% of the total latency. In some scenarios, the GPU is ready to process the next batch of tasks, while the CPU is still waiting for tool calls to return. Another key factor is the rapid expansion of the context window. In 2024, most mainstream models supported 128K to 200K tokens. Entering 2025, models such as Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4.1, and Llama 4 Maverick began supporting over 1 million tokens. The KV cache (used to accelerate the inference process of Transformers models) grows linearly with the number of tokens, reaching approximately 200GB at 1 million tokens, far exceeding the 80GB VRAM capacity of a single H100 GPU. One solution to this problem is to offload part of the KV cache to CPU memory. This means that the CPU not only manages orchestration and tool calls but also assists in handling data that cannot be stored in VRAM. CPU memory capacity, memory bandwidth, and the interconnect speed between the CPU and GPU become crucial to system performance. Therefore, CPUs suitable for the era of intelligent agents require low latency, consistent memory access capabilities, and stronger system-level coordination capabilities, rather than simply expanding the scale of a single core. What are the manufacturers doing? Some are grabbing market share, others are redesigning. Faced with this sudden surge in CPU demand, the major manufacturers are employing completely different strategies. Intel is the traditional leader in server CPUs. Data from Mercury Research shows that in the fourth quarter of 2025, Intel still held a 60% share of the server CPU market, AMD held 24.3%, and Nvidia held 6.2%.
