However, Intel has been chasing new technologies for years, and this surge in CPU demand presents both an opportunity and a test for them. Intel's current strategy is two-pronged: On one hand, it continues to sell Xeon processors, deeply binding itself to massive customers like Google; on the other hand, it collaborates with SambaNova to launch a combined solution based on Xeon processors and its self-developed RDU accelerator, emphasizing the selling point of "running intelligent agent inference without a GPU." The Xeon 6 Granite Rapids and 18A process roadmap will be key to whether Intel can turn the tide. AMD, on the other hand, is one of the biggest beneficiaries of this surge in CPU demand. In the fourth quarter of 2025, AMD's data center revenue reached $5.4 billion, a year-on-year increase of 39%. The fifth-generation EPYC Turin accounted for more than half of server CPU revenue, and cloud instance deployments running EPYC grew by more than 50% year-over-year. AMD's server CPU revenue share surpassed 40% for the first time. AMD CEO Lisa Su attributed the growth directly to the development of "agents"—agent workloads have "pushed" tasks back onto traditional CPU tasks. In February 2026, AMD also announced a potential deal with Meta worth over $100 billion to supply MI450 GPUs and Venice EPYC CPUs. However, AMD still has room for improvement in system-level collaboration, lacking mature high-speed CPU-GPU interconnect capabilities like NVLink C2C. As agent systems increasingly demand higher efficiency in data interaction and collaboration, the importance of this aspect is gradually rising. Nvidia's approach to CPU design is completely different from Intel's and AMD's. Nvidia's Grace CPU has only 72 cores, while AMD EPYC and Intel Xeon typically have 128. Dion Harris, Nvidia's head of AI infrastructure, explained, "If you're a hyperscale enterprise, you want to maximize the number of cores per CPU, which basically reduces costs—the cost per core in dollars. So it's a business model." In other words, in the AI ​​computing power system, the CPU's role is no longer that of the main general-purpose computing power, but rather a "scheduling hub" serving the GPU. If the CPU can't keep up, the expensive GPU will be forced to wait, resulting in a decrease in overall efficiency. Therefore, Nvidia prioritizes efficient collaboration between the CPU and GPU in its design. For example, NVLink C2C interconnect increases the bandwidth between the CPU and GPU to approximately 1.8TB/s, far exceeding traditional PCIe. The CPU can directly access GPU memory, significantly simplifying KV cache management. Currently, NVIDIA is selling the Vera CPU as a standalone product. CoreWeave is its first customer. The deal with Meta is even more significant, marking its first large-scale "pure Grace deployment"—a large-scale independent deployment of CPUs without GPU pairing. Ben Bajarin, principal analyst at research firm Creative Strategies, points out that in high-intensity system collaboration, the CPU's processing power must match the accelerator's iteration speed. Even a one percent delay in the data channel can drastically reduce the economic efficiency of the entire AI cluster. This pursuit of ultimate system efficiency is forcing all major manufacturers to re-evaluate CPU performance metrics. Holger Mueller, Vice President and Principal Analyst at Constellation Research, stated that as AI workloads shift towards agent-driven architectures, the CPU is becoming increasingly central. He pointed out, "In the world of agents, agents need to call APIs and various business applications, tasks best suited for CPUs." He added, "Currently, there's no definitive answer as to whether GPUs or CPUs are better suited for inference tasks. GPUs have an advantage in model training, while custom ASICs like TPUs have their strengths. But one thing is clear: Google needs a hybrid processor architecture. Therefore, Google's choice to partner with Intel makes sense." Conclusion: In the Era of Intelligent Agents, the Balance of Computing Power Shifts Backwards. In the latest industry observations, one piece of data deserves our attention. In the $38 billion cooperation agreement between Amazon AWS and OpenAI, the official statement explicitly mentioned an expansion scale of "tens of millions of CPUs." In the past few years, the industry's focus has typically been on "hundreds of thousands of GPUs." However, leading labs like OpenAI are proactively using CPU scale as a key planning variable, sending a clear signal: the expansion of intelligent agent workloads must be built upon a massive CPU infrastructure. Bank of America predicts that by 2030, the global CPU market size is expected to double from the current $27 billion to $60 billion. Almost all of this additional market share will be driven by AI. We are witnessing the expansion of a completely new infrastructure: major manufacturers are no longer just piling on GPUs, but simultaneously expanding an entire layer of "CPU scheduling infrastructure" specifically to provide operational support for AI agents. The collaboration between Intel and Google, and Musk's heavy investment in custom chips, all demonstrate one fact: the winning factor in the AI ​​race is shifting forward. When computing power is no longer scarce, whoever can solve the system-level "bottleneck" first will be the one to emerge victorious in this trillion-dollar game.
